severity_adjustments: seedloader-002 downgraded from CRITICAL to HIGH — internal seed data is not user-controlled input, so KeyError on malformed data is a robustness issue, not a security issue

findings.title: SeedDataLoader class has zero callers — entire module is dead code
findings.problem: The entire SeedDataLoader class (149 lines) is never instantiated anywhere in the codebase. grep for 'SeedDataLoader' across all .py files returns only the class definition in seed_loader.py. This means sync(), _import_doc(), and _delete_seed_doc() are unreachable. All JSON file I/O (seed_manifest.json, seed_chunks.json, seed_state.json) is never exercised at runtime. The seed data files shipped alongside the application are never loaded.
findings.fix: Either wire SeedDataLoader into the application initialization flow (e.g., call seed_loader.sync() in main.py or api_server.py lifespan), or remove seed_loader.py and all associated seed data files if seed loading is not needed.
findings.evidence: grep 'SeedDataLoader' → 1 match (class definition only); grep 'seed_loader' → 0 callers

findings.title: Direct dict key access on unvalidated JSON — KeyError on malformed seed data
findings.problem: In _import_doc(), line 116 accesses c['text'] and line 117 accesses c['embedding'] via direct subscript without validation. If the seed chunks JSON has a malformed entry (e.g., missing 'text' or 'embedding' key), this raises an unhandled KeyError that aborts the entire sync() operation with a confusing traceback. vector_store.add_chunks_with_embeddings() validates embedding presence (lines 342-348), but seed_loader bypasses all validation.
findings.fix: Replace c['text'] with c.get('text', '') and c['embedding'] with c.get('embedding'). Add validation: skip entries with missing required fields and log a WARNING.

findings.title: BM25 index not cleaned up on seed doc deletion — stale search results
findings.problem: When _delete_seed_doc() removes chunks from ChromaDB (line 142), the corresponding BM25 index entries are NOT removed. Subsequent hybrid search queries will return stale results pointing to deleted chunks. The BM25Index class has no remove_document or remove_by_prefix method, so there is no way to clean up without rebuilding the entire index.
findings.fix: After _delete_seed_doc(), trigger a full BM25 index rebuild from remaining ChromaDB entries. Alternatively, add a remove_by_prefix() method to BM25Index.
findings.evidence: Lines 84, 145-149 document the gap; BM25Index has no removal API

findings.title: chunk_id format mismatch between import and deletion logic
findings.problem: _import_doc() generates fallback chunk_ids as f'{doc_id}_chunk_{i}' (line 110). _delete_seed_doc() searches for IDs matching prefix 'seed_{doc_id}_' (line 138). The deletion prefix expects IDs starting with 'seed_' but the fallback generation uses the raw doc_id. If doc_id doesn't start with 'seed_', the deletion prefix will never match the stored chunk_ids, causing orphan chunks to accumulate in ChromaDB. The actual behavior depends entirely on the JSON's 'chunk_id' field which may or may not follow either convention.
findings.fix: Standardize chunk_id format: use a consistent prefix convention (e.g., always 'seed_{doc_id}_chunk_{i}') and ensure deletion prefix matches. Or track stored chunk_ids and delete by exact ID.
findings.evidence:
Line 110: chunk_id = c.get('chunk_id') or f'{doc_id}_chunk_{i}'
Line 138: prefix = f'seed_{doc_id}_'

findings.title: No schema validation on parsed JSON — silent wrong-type handling
findings.problem: Lines 45, 50, 59 call json.load() and use results directly without schema validation. If manifest is a dict instead of a list, line 68 'for entry in manifest' iterates over dict keys instead of entries — silently importing nothing. If all_chunks is a dict keyed by doc_id instead of a flat list, the filter at line 108 fails silently. No type checking, no required-key validation.
findings.fix: Add schema validation after each json.load(): assert isinstance(manifest, list), validate required keys, validate all_chunks structure.
findings.evidence: Lines 45, 50, 59: json.load() with no validation; Line 68: 'for entry in manifest' assumes list
findings.ai_pattern: Schema mismatch / Silent failure on wrong data type

findings.title: Version comparison without type safety — potential TypeError
findings.problem: Line 82 performs 'state[doc_id] < version' without ensuring both values are comparable types. If seed_state.json contains version as a string ('1.0') while seed_manifest.json has version as an integer (1), Python 3 raises TypeError. No type coercion or normalization.
findings.fix: Normalize versions to consistent type (e.g., int or tuple) before comparison. Add try/except TypeError with fallback logic.

findings.title: False state recording when zero chunks imported
findings.problem: When _import_doc() succeeds but produces no chunks (empty doc_chunks), the doc_id is still added to state['imported'] and state[doc_id] = version is set (lines 80-81). This records a false success in seed_state.json, preventing re-import on subsequent sync() calls. The actual check at line 122 returns empty list silently without logging.
findings.fix: Wrap _import_doc() result: if doc_chunks is empty, skip state update and log WARNING. Do not add to 'imported' list.
findings.evidence: Lines 79-81: state[doc_id] = version set unconditionally; Lines 122-123: silent empty return
findings.ai_pattern: Happy-path-only error handling / False state recording

findings.title: State file write has no atomicity — corruption risk on crash
findings.problem: Line 93 writes seed_state.json using json.dump() without write-then-rename pattern. If the process crashes mid-write, the state file is left corrupted. Next sync() fails to json.load() it, falls back to state = {}, losing all seed tracking and causing full re-import.
findings.fix: Use atomic write: write to temp file, then os.replace() to target path.

findings.title: Metadata shallow copy — nested dict mutations may leak
findings.problem: Line 111 does metadata = dict(c.get('metadata', {})), a shallow copy. If metadata contains nested dicts/lists, modifications (line 113: metadata['doc_id'] = doc_id) affect the original in-memory seed data. If the same seed data is reused across multiple sync() calls, mutations accumulate.
findings.fix: Use copy.deepcopy(metadata) instead of dict() for proper deep cloning.
